Lead development, multi-step marketing, arena, rather than in the one step we only get by now customers where most people live. You talk a lot about, and again, this is in here for you to model, so you talk about omnipresence, and you mentioned it a couple of times, but let's sum it up. What exactly is omnipresence? You're there in front of your customer, or you're developing prospects, but for the sake of referrals in front of your customer, so that they can't go a day, the goal, they can't go a day, and they can't turn it in a 360 degree circle without bumping into you. That's the goal. So if you think about somebody who is a Amazon Prime customer, they buy more and more and more and more stuff from Amazon and less and less and less stuff from every place else. So pretty soon, they can't really turn a full circle around in their house without seeing stuff they bought from Amazon, which reminds them, oh shit, I can buy this from Amazon, I've got to go to a store, I've got to, hey, I can get diapers, I can get whatever, Amazon shipping, elephants and elephant manure and everything in between. And they think about it a lot because Amazon communicates with them a lot. If you think about Disney, a Disney fan, you cannot be in their house, they will not turn around 360 degrees and not bump into Disney. And Disney is communicating with them every single damn day. Broad in general, turn on the TV and click one night and try not to bump into a Disney movie, a Disney show, a Disney ad, a Disney something. They integrate Disney into plot lines. So the shows, they own ABC. So ABC, stuff happens, they go to Disney. They own ESPN. What does everybody say when they win this? I'm going to Disney. So it's integrated on a broad level and then it's integrated in your own environment. Kids got stuffed animals and videos. I got the D23 thing on the wall over here. There's a reason they do, they have, I'm going to mess the number up. It's over 300,000 product skews at retail of Disney products. So there's dog dishes. There's utensils. There's galoshes. Does everybody still say galoshes? There's coffee. There's, you name it. So you're going to have some Disney product around you if you're a Disney fan at all. And so the goal is the same. So if you look at GKIC and you clock a diamond member and you figure up every communication, non-sales communication and sales communication. You can separate them if you wish. If you just take the non-sales communications, it's 280 something a year. There's already 365 days. If you throw in the sales stuff, it's more like a couple thousand. So there's not a day going by that we're not like reminding you. If you walk into a good customer's office or house, you can't turn around 360 degrees without seeing stuff. They got all the Dan Kennedy books in one place on a shelf and they got the Dan Kennedy bobbleheads sitting in front of it. And they got a cup. And they got their audio program, their home study court, they got those over here. They got mouse pads. They got posters. They got stuff. And most people have in their offices, Grant Miller calls it his Dan Kennedy shrine. It's two big bookcases full of everything. And so you're showing up broadly, books, webcasts, etc. You're showing up direct and you're showing up often, often, often, often. So what are the obvious benefits? Are there any other benefits on the presence other than just people are going to see you so they're going to refer? It affects referrals. In retention businesses, it affects retention. It can affect cumulative consumption. It can reflect, it can affect even satisfaction. How? Well, we're more...
